q: Two satellites <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> having masses in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math> are revolving in circular orbits of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>r</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>r</mi></math> respectively around the earth. The ratio of total mechanical energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is
question: Two satellites <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> having masses in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math> are revolving in circular orbits of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>r</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>r</mi></math> respectively around the earth. The ratio of total mechanical energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is
solution:
Given that m1 m2=43,r1r2=34

Now the total energy of a satellite TE=12mvo2+-GMmr

Since the orbital speed of a satellite vo=GMr

⇒TE=-GMm2r∝mr

⇒TE1TE2=m1 m2·r2r1=43×43=169

subject: Physics
q: The time period of a satellite of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> hours. If the separation between the earth and the satellite is decreased to one fourth of the previous value, then its new time period will become.
question: The time period of a satellite of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> hours. If the separation between the earth and the satellite is decreased to one fourth of the previous value, then its new time period will become.
solution:
From Kepler's law of periods, we know

T2∝R3

⇒T12T22=R13R23⇒T1T22=RR43

⇒T12T22=64

∴T2=248=3 hours

subject: Physics
q: A body is projected vertically upwards from the surface of earth with a velocity sufficient enough to carry it to infinity. The time taken by it to reach height h is           S.
question: A body is projected vertically upwards from the surface of earth with a velocity sufficient enough to carry it to infinity. The time taken by it to reach height h is           S.
solution:
Applying energy conservation from (1 to 2,
12 m·2GMRe-GMmRe=12 mv2-GMmR+r

⇒12mv2=GMmR+r

⇒v=2GMR+r=drdt

⇒ 2GM∫0tdt=∫ReRe+h(R+r)dr

2GM·t=23(R+r)32ReRe+h

t=23Re32GM1+hRe32-1

GMRe2=g

t=132Re g1+hRe32-1

subject: Physics
q: Three identical particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> each are placed in a straight line with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The gravitational force on a fourth particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> of the same mass is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>, when placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> on the perpendicular bisector of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> will be approximately
question: Three identical particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> each are placed in a straight line with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The gravitational force on a fourth particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> of the same mass is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>, when placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> on the perpendicular bisector of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> will be approximately
solution:
Given here, m=100 kg

Gravitational force, FAP=Gm21322,

FBP=Gm2132 and FCP=Gm21322

Net gravitational force, 

Fnet=FBP+FAPcos45°+FCPcos45°

 =G×1041321+12

F≃100G

subject: Physics
q: The gravitational potential at a point above the surface of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the acceleration due to gravity at that point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Assume that the mean radius of earth to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. The height of this point above the earth's surface is:
question: The gravitational potential at a point above the surface of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the acceleration due to gravity at that point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Assume that the mean radius of earth to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. The height of this point above the earth's surface is:
solution:
Gravitational potential at a distance r from the centre of the earth is given by, V=-GMr.

Therefore, we can write

-GMERE+h=-5.12×107    …i

also acceleration due to gravity at that point will be

GMERE+h2=6.4    …ii

By (i) and (ii), we get

-GMERE+hGMERE+h2=5.12×1076.4

⇒RE+h=80×105 m=8000 km⇒h=8000 km-6400 km=1600 km

subject: Physics
q:
A satellite of $10^3 \mathrm{~kg}$ mass is revolving in circular orbit of radius $2 R$. If $\frac{10^4 R}{6} J$ energy is supplied to the satellite, it would revolve in a new circular orbit of radius
(use $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, R=$ radius of earth)
question:
A satellite of $10^3 \mathrm{~kg}$ mass is revolving in circular orbit of radius $2 R$. If $\frac{10^4 R}{6} J$ energy is supplied to the satellite, it would revolve in a new circular orbit of radius
(use $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, R=$ radius of earth)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Total energy }=\frac{-\mathrm{GMm}}{2(2 \mathrm{R})} \\ & \text { if energy }=\frac{10^4 \mathrm{R}}{6} \text { is added then } \\ & \frac{-\mathrm{GMm}}{4 \mathrm{R}}+\frac{10^4 \mathrm{R}}{6}=\frac{-\mathrm{GMm}}{2 \mathrm{r}} \\ & \text { where } \mathrm{r} \text { is new radius of revolving and } \mathrm{g}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}^2} \\ & -\frac{\mathrm{mgR}}{4}+\frac{10^4 \mathrm{R}}{6}=-\frac{\mathrm{mgR}^2}{2 \mathrm{r}}\left(\mathrm{m}=10^3 \mathrm{~kg}\right) \\ & -\frac{10^3 \times 10 \times \mathrm{R}}{4}+\frac{10^4 \mathrm{R}}{6}=-\frac{10^3 \times 10 \times \mathrm{R}^2}{2 \mathrm{r}} \\ & -\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{6}=-\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2 \mathrm{r}} \\ & \mathrm{r}=6 \mathrm{R}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Assume there are two identical simple pendulum Clocks-1 is placed on the earth and Clock-2 is placed on a space station located at a height h above the earth surface. Clock-1 and Clock-2 operate at time periods 4 s and 6 s respectively. Then the value of h is -
(consider radius of earth RE=6400 km and g on earth 10 m s-2)
question:
Assume there are two identical simple pendulum Clocks-1 is placed on the earth and Clock-2 is placed on a space station located at a height h above the earth surface. Clock-1 and Clock-2 operate at time periods 4 s and 6 s respectively. Then the value of h is -
(consider radius of earth RE=6400 km and g on earth 10 m s-2)
solution:
The time period of a simple pendulum is given by, T=2πlg.

Therefore, T∝1g.

The acceleration due to gravity at height h from the surface of the earth is given by, g'=gR+h2

Therefore,

T1T2=g2g1⇒46=RR+h⇒h=R2=3200 km

subject: Physics
q: A small point of mass $m$ is placed at a distance $2 R$ from the centre ' $O^{\prime}$ of a big uniform solid sphere of mass M and radius R . The gravitational force on ' m ' due to M is $\mathrm{F}_1$. A spherical part of radius $\mathrm{R} / 3$ is removed from the big sphere as shown in the figure and the gravitational force on m due to remaining part of M is found to be $\mathrm{F}_2$. The value of ratio $\mathrm{F}_1: \mathrm{F}_2$ is
question: A small point of mass $m$ is placed at a distance $2 R$ from the centre ' $O^{\prime}$ of a big uniform solid sphere of mass M and radius R . The gravitational force on ' m ' due to M is $\mathrm{F}_1$. A spherical part of radius $\mathrm{R} / 3$ is removed from the big sphere as shown in the figure and the gravitational force on m due to remaining part of M is found to be $\mathrm{F}_2$. The value of ratio $\mathrm{F}_1: \mathrm{F}_2$ is
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{F}_1=\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{(2 \mathrm{R})^2} .....(1)\\
& \mathrm{~F}_2=\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{(2 \mathrm{R})^2}-\left(\frac{\mathrm{G}\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}}{27}\right) \mathrm{m}}{\left(\frac{4 \mathrm{R}}{3}\right)^2}\right) \\
& \mathrm{F}_2=\frac{11}{48} \frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{\mathrm{R}^2} ....(2)\\
& \mathrm{~F}_1: \mathrm{F}_2=12: 11
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The mass density of a spherical galaxy varies as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="italic">K</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">r</mi></mfrac></math> over a large distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> from its center. In that region, a small star is in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. Then the period of revolution,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> depends on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> as:
question: The mass density of a spherical galaxy varies as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="italic">K</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">r</mi></mfrac></math> over a large distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> from its center. In that region, a small star is in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. Then the period of revolution,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> depends on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> as:
solution:
M=∫ρdV

M=∫0Rkr4πr2dr

M=4πk∫0Rrdr

M=4πkR22=2πkR2

FG=GMmR2=mω02R

⇒  G4πkR22R2=ω02R  ⇒ω0=2πkGR

∵T=2πω0=2πR2πkG=2πRkG

⇒T2∝R

subject: Physics
q:
Match List‐I (Event) with List‐II (Order of the time interval for the happening of the event) and select the correct option from the options given below the lists.

  List-I   List-II
(a) The rotation period of earth (i) 105 s
(b) Revolution period of earth  (ii) 107 s
(c) Period of a light wave  (iii) 10-15 s
(d) Period of a sound wave  (iv) 10-3 s
question:
Match List‐I (Event) with List‐II (Order of the time interval for the happening of the event) and select the correct option from the options given below the lists.

  List-I   List-II
(a) The rotation period of earth (i) 105 s
(b) Revolution period of earth  (ii) 107 s
(c) Period of a light wave  (iii) 10-15 s
(d) Period of a sound wave  (iv) 10-3 s
solution:
The duration of the day 86400 s≃105 s

revolution period 365 days≃107 s

For the light of λ=6000 A0, time period≃10-15 s and

for the sound wave time period≃10-3 s

subject: Physics
q: The orbital angular momentum of a satellite is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math>, when it is revolving in a circular orbit at height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> from earth surface. If the distance of satellite from the earth centre is increased by eight times to its initial value, then the new angular momentum will be
question: The orbital angular momentum of a satellite is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math>, when it is revolving in a circular orbit at height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> from earth surface. If the distance of satellite from the earth centre is increased by eight times to its initial value, then the new angular momentum will be
solution:
For a satellite revolving around the Earth in a circular orbit, it can be written that

mv2r=GMmr2   ...1 (r=R+h is the distance from the centre of the Earth)

Simplify equation (1) to obtain the linear speed of the satellite.

v=GMr

Thus, the angular momentum of the satellite is given by

L=mvr=mrGMr=mGMr   ...2

Similarly, for distance r' from the centre of the Earth, the angular momentum is given by

L'=mGMr'   ...3

Divide equation (3) by equation (2) to obtain the required angular momentum.

L'L=mGMr'mGMr= r'r   ...4

Now, r'=r+8r=9r

Substitute the known values of the parameters into equation (4) to obtain the required angular momentum.

L'L=9⇒L'=3L

subject: Physics
q: The value of the acceleration due to gravity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>g</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of the earth) from the surface of the earth. It is again equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>g</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> at a depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> below the surface the earth. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>d</mi><mi>R</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math> equals:
question: The value of the acceleration due to gravity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>g</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of the earth) from the surface of the earth. It is again equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>g</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> at a depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> below the surface the earth. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>d</mi><mi>R</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math> equals:
solution:
Given that

gn=gd

GM(R+h)2=GMR3(R-d)

GM(R+R/2)2=GMR3(R-d)

4GM9R2=GMR21-dR

49=1-dR

dR=1-49=59

d=59R

subject: Physics
q:
If the acceleration due to gravity experienced by a point mass at a height h above the surface of earth is same as that of the acceleration due to gravity at a depth αhh≪Re ) from the earth surface. The value of α will be _____ .

(use Re=6400 km)
question:
If the acceleration due to gravity experienced by a point mass at a height h above the surface of earth is same as that of the acceleration due to gravity at a depth αhh≪Re ) from the earth surface. The value of α will be _____ .

(use Re=6400 km)
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at a height h above the earth's surface is given by g1-2hRe 

And acceleration due to gravity at depth d below the earth surface is given by g1-dRe.

As gabove=gbelow

So, g1-2hRe=g1-dRe

⇒2hRe=dRe

⇒αh=d

Thus, the value of α=2.

subject: Physics
q: A satellite of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> about the center of the earth. A meteorite of the same mass, falling towards the earth, collides with the satellite completely inelastic. The speeds of the satellite and the meteorite are the same, just before the collision. The subsequent motion of the combined body will be:
question: A satellite of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> about the center of the earth. A meteorite of the same mass, falling towards the earth, collides with the satellite completely inelastic. The speeds of the satellite and the meteorite are the same, just before the collision. The subsequent motion of the combined body will be:
options: Such that it escapes to infinity
In a circular orbit of a different radius
In the same circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math>
solution:
2mvx=mv

vx=v2

Similarly vy=v2

Vnet=v22+v22=v2
As resultant velocity after collision is less than orbital velocity and it's direction is neither radial nor tangential , the combined mass will follow elliptical orbit

subject: Physics
q: A simple pendulum doing small oscillations at a place $\mathrm{R}$ height above earth surface has time period of $T_1=4 \mathrm{~s}$. $T_2$ would be it's time period if it is brought to a point which is at a height $2 \mathrm{R}$ from earth surface. Choose the correct relation $[R=$ radius of earth $]$ :
question: A simple pendulum doing small oscillations at a place $\mathrm{R}$ height above earth surface has time period of $T_1=4 \mathrm{~s}$. $T_2$ would be it's time period if it is brought to a point which is at a height $2 \mathrm{R}$ from earth surface. Choose the correct relation $[R=$ radius of earth $]$ :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}_1=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\ell}{\mathrm{GM}}(2 \mathrm{R})^2} \\ & \mathrm{~T}_2=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\ell}{\mathrm{GM}}(3 \mathrm{R})^2} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\frac{2}{3}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : Rotation of the earth shows effect on the value of acceleration due to gravity (g).

Statement II : The effect of rotation of the earth on the value of g at the equator is minimum and that at the pole is maximum.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : Rotation of the earth shows effect on the value of acceleration due to gravity (g).

Statement II : The effect of rotation of the earth on the value of g at the equator is minimum and that at the pole is maximum.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Statement I is false but statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but statement II is false
solution:
The variation of acceleration due to gravity with the rotation of Earth can be written as

g'=g-ω2Rcos2λ   ...1

Substitute 0° for λ for equatorial position to obtain the acceleration due to gravity.

g'=g-ω2Rcos20°= g-ω2R

Substitute 90° for λ for polar position to obtain the acceleration due to gravity.

g'=g-ω2Rcos290°=g

The above two equations show that the effect on acceleration due to gravity is maximum at the equator and minimum at pole.

subject: Physics
q: Two satellites of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> revolve around the earth in circular orbits of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> respectively. The ratio of orbital speeds of the satellites respectively is
question: Two satellites of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> revolve around the earth in circular orbits of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> respectively. The ratio of orbital speeds of the satellites respectively is
solution:
The orbital velocity is given 

V=GMr

where M is the mass of earth and r is the radius of the orbit of satellite.

The velocity of the satellite with mass m is 

V1=GMr

and the velocity of the satellite with mass 3m is 

V2=GM3r

Hence, the ratio of the velocities is 

V1V2=3rr=31

subject: Physics
q:
Every planet revolves around the sun in an elliptical orbit :

A. The force acting on a planet is inversely proportional to square of distance from sun.

B. Force acting on planet is inversely proportional to product of the masses of the planet and the sun.

C. The centripetal force acting on the planet is directed away from the sun.

D. The square of time period of revolution of planet around sun is directly proportional to cube of semi-major axis of elliptical orbit.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Every planet revolves around the sun in an elliptical orbit :

A. The force acting on a planet is inversely proportional to square of distance from sun.

B. Force acting on planet is inversely proportional to product of the masses of the planet and the sun.

C. The centripetal force acting on the planet is directed away from the sun.

D. The square of time period of revolution of planet around sun is directly proportional to cube of semi-major axis of elliptical orbit.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Newton's law of gravitation state that gravitational force between two point masses is directly proportional to the product of their masses m1 and m2 and inversely proportional to the square of the distance r between them. It is stated as F=Gm1m2r2

A planet moves around the sun in a circular path for which the gravitational force of attraction on the planet by the sun provides the necessary centripetal force. This centripetal force is always directed towards the centre of the sun at each point of its path which is responsible for circular motion of planet.

From Kepler's Third Law, we know that the squares of the orbital periods of the planets are directly proportional to the cubes of the semi-major axes of their orbits.

⇒T2∝a3 

Thus, A and D only are correct.

orbital velocity = GMr

Ve=2v

question: In the reported figure of earth, the value of acceleration due to gravity is same at point A and C but it is smaller than that of its value at point B (surface of the earth). The value of OA:AB will be x:5. The value of x is

subject: Physics
q: A planet $(P_1)$ is moving around the star of mass $2M$ in the orbit of radius $R$. Another planet $(P_2)$ is moving around another star of mass $4M$ in a orbit of radius $2R$. Ratio of time periods of revolution of $P_2$ and $P_1$ is _______.
question: A planet $(P_1)$ is moving around the star of mass $2M$ in the orbit of radius $R$. Another planet $(P_2)$ is moving around another star of mass $4M$ in a orbit of radius $2R$. Ratio of time periods of revolution of $P_2$ and $P_1$ is _______.
solution:
Using Kepler's third law, the time period of revolution of a planet is given by $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{r^3}{GM_s}}$.

For planet $P_1$, the orbital radius is $r = R$ and the mass of the star is $M_s = 2M$:
$T_1 = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{R^3}{G(2M)}}$

For planet $P_2$, the orbital radius is $r = 2R$ and the mass of the star is $M_s = 4M$:
$T_2 = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{(2R)^3}{G(4M)}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{8R^3}{4GM}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2R^3}{GM}}$

Taking the ratio of $T_2$ and $T_1$:
$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\dfrac{2R^3}{GM}}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{R^3}{2GM}}} = \sqrt{2 \times 2} = 2$

Answer: $2$

subject: Physics
q: A satellite is launched into a circular orbit of radius ' R ' around the earth. A second satellite is launched into an orbit of radius 1.03 R . The time period of revolution of the second satellite is larger than the first one approximately by
question: A satellite is launched into a circular orbit of radius ' R ' around the earth. A second satellite is launched into an orbit of radius 1.03 R . The time period of revolution of the second satellite is larger than the first one approximately by
solution: $T_1 \propto(R)^{3 / 2}$ and $T_2 \propto(1.03 R)^{3 / 2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad T_2=(1.03 R)^{3 / 2} \cdot T_1 \approx 1.045 T_1$ So $T_2$ will larger by $4.5 \%$ w.r.t. $T_1$.

q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I : For a planet, if the ratio of mass of the planet to its radius increase, the escape velocity from the planet also increase.

Statement II : Escape velocity is independent of the radius of the planet.

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I : For a planet, if the ratio of mass of the planet to its radius increase, the escape velocity from the planet also increase.

Statement II : Escape velocity is independent of the radius of the planet.

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Statement I is correct but statement II is incorrect
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
solution:
The escape velocity is given by the formula

vesc =2GMR

Clearly, the escape velocity depends on the mass and radius of the planet. Hence, if the ratio increases the escape velocity also increases.

So, Statement I is correct while statement II is wrong.

subject: Physics
q: Two spherical bodies of mass $M$ and $5 M \&$ radii $R \& 2 R$ respectively are released in free space with initial separation between their centres equal to $12 \mathrm{R}$. If they attract each other due to gravitational force only, then the distance covered by the smaller body just before collision is
question: Two spherical bodies of mass $M$ and $5 M \&$ radii $R \& 2 R$ respectively are released in free space with initial separation between their centres equal to $12 \mathrm{R}$. If they attract each other due to gravitational force only, then the distance covered by the smaller body just before collision is
solution:
Distance between the surface of the spherical bodies $=12 R-R-2 R=9 R$
Force $\propto$ Mass, $\quad$ Acceleration $\propto$ Mass, $\quad$ Distance $\propto$ Acceleration
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{a}_1}{\mathrm{a}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{SM}}=\frac{1}{5} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{S}_1}{\mathrm{~S}_2}=\frac{1}{5} \Rightarrow \mathrm{S}_2=5 \mathrm{~S}_1 \\
& \mathrm{~S}_1+\mathrm{S}_2=9 \Rightarrow 6 \mathrm{~S}_1=9 \Rightarrow \mathrm{S}_1=\frac{9}{6}=1.5,
\end{aligned}
$$
Note: Maximum distance will be travelled by smaller bodies due to the greater acceleration caused by the same gravitational force

solution:
Usurface +E1=Uh

KE of satellite is zero at earth surface and at height h

-GMemRe+E1=-GMemRe+h

E1=GMem1Re-1Re+h

E1=GMemRe+h×hRe

Gravitational attraction FG=maC=mv2Re+h

⇒mv2Re+h=GMemRe+h2

mv2=GMemRe+h

E2=mv22=GMem2Re+h

E1=E2

hRe=12⇒h=Re2=3200 km

subject: Physics
q: If the radius of earth shrinks by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> while its mass remains same. The acceleration due to gravity on the earth's surface will approximately
question: If the radius of earth shrinks by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> while its mass remains same. The acceleration due to gravity on the earth's surface will approximately
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the earth is given by, g=GMR2.

The radius shrunk by 2%. Therefore, ∆RR×100=-2%.

As the mass is constant and the change in the radius of the earth is relatively small, we can write

∆gg=-2∆RR⇒∆gg×100=4%

Hence, the acceleration due to gravity increases by 4%.

question: The gravitational field in a region is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>g</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mover><mtext>i</mtext><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>12</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The change in the gravitational potential energy of a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> when it is taken from the origin to a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
dV=-g→.dr→

⇒∆V=-g→.∆r→
             =-5i^+12j^·7i^-3j^
              = - 3 5 - 3 6 = 1

⇒ΔU=mΔV=2J

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: If we move from poles to equator, the direction of acceleration due to gravity of earth always points towards the center of earth without any variation in its magnitude.

Reason R: At equator, the direction of acceleration due to the gravity is towards the center of earth.

In the light of above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: If we move from poles to equator, the direction of acceleration due to gravity of earth always points towards the center of earth without any variation in its magnitude.

Reason R: At equator, the direction of acceleration due to the gravity is towards the center of earth.

In the light of above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Assertion and reason both are correct and reason is correct explanation of assertion.
Assertion and reason both are correct but reason is not correct explanation of assertion.
Assertion is true but reason is false
Assertion is false but reason is true
solution:
Value of effective g increases as we move from the equator to pole because on the equator its value is less due to earth's rotational motion and consequent centrifugal force. 
Moreover, the equator of the earth is at a larger distance from the centre of the earth as compared to the poles. This is another reason why g is greater on the pole than the equator.

At the equator, direction of gravitational pull and centrifugal force are in same line, hence net force is towards the centre of the Earth.

subject: Physics
q: Two hypothetical planets of masses $\mathrm{m}_1$ and $\mathrm{m}_2$ are at rest when they are infinite distance apart. Because of the gravitational force they move towards each other along the line joining their centres. What is their speed when their separation is ' $d$ '? (Speed of $\mathrm{m}_1$ is $\mathrm{v}_1$ and that of $\mathrm{m}_2$ is $\mathrm{v}_2$ )
question: Two hypothetical planets of masses $\mathrm{m}_1$ and $\mathrm{m}_2$ are at rest when they are infinite distance apart. Because of the gravitational force they move towards each other along the line joining their centres. What is their speed when their separation is ' $d$ '? (Speed of $\mathrm{m}_1$ is $\mathrm{v}_1$ and that of $\mathrm{m}_2$ is $\mathrm{v}_2$ )
solution:
We choose reference point, infinity, where total energy of the system is zero.
So, initial energy of the system $=0$
Final energy
$$
=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1^2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_2 \mathrm{v}_2^2-\frac{\mathrm{Gm}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2}{\mathrm{~d}}
$$
From conservation of energy, Initial energy $=$ Final energy
$$
\therefore 0=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1{ }^2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_2 \mathrm{v}_2{ }^2-\frac{\mathrm{Gm}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2}{\mathrm{~d}}
$$
or $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1^2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_1 \mathrm{v}_2^2=\frac{\mathrm{Gm}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2}{\mathrm{~d}} \ldots(1)$
By conservation of linear momentum
$$
\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{v}_2=0
$$
or $\frac{\mathrm{v}_1}{\mathrm{v}_2}=-\frac{\mathrm{m}_2}{\mathrm{~m}_1} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}_2=-\frac{\mathrm{m}_1}{\mathrm{~m}_2} \mathrm{v}_1$
Putting value of $\mathrm{v}_2$ in equation (1), we get
$$
\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1^2+\mathrm{m}_2\left(-\frac{\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1}{\mathrm{~m}_2}\right)^2=\frac{2 \mathrm{Gm}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2}{\mathrm{~d}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{m_1 m_2 v_1^2+m_1^2 v_1^2}{m_2}=\frac{2 \mathrm{Gm}_1 m_2}{\mathrm{~d}} \\
&\mathrm{v}_1=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{Gm}_2^2}{\mathrm{~d}\left(\mathrm{~m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2\right)}}=\mathrm{m}_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{d}\left(\mathrm{m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2\right)}} \\
&\text { Similarly } \mathrm{v}_2=-\mathrm{m}_1 \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{d}\left(\mathrm{m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2\right)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q:
A body is projected vertically upwards from the surface of earth with a velocity equal to one third of escape velocity. The maximum height attained by the body will be
(Take radius of earth =6400 km and g=10 ms-2)
question:
A body is projected vertically upwards from the surface of earth with a velocity equal to one third of escape velocity. The maximum height attained by the body will be
(Take radius of earth =6400 km and g=10 ms-2)
solution:
Total mechanical energy of the body at the surface of earth

Mi=-GMmR+12m132GMR2=-GMmR+GMm9R=-8GMm9R

Total mechanical energy of the body at the highest point

Mf=-GMmR+h

Using conservation of mechanical energy,

Mi=Mf⇒-GMmR+h=-8GMm9R

⇒R+h=9R8

⇒h=R8=64008=800 km
